title: Identifying Propositions
description: Identify valid logical propositions from natural language sentences.
example_task: Select the valid proposition: A) 'Close the door.' B) 'The door is closed.' C) 'Is the door closed?'

title: Understanding Truth Values
description: Assign the fundamental states of reality (True or False) to isolated atomic propositions.
example_task: If proposition P is 'Paris is the capital of France', what is the determinate truth value of P?

title: The Negation Operator
description: Apply the tilde (~) to a proposition to invert its truth value, recognizing it as a unary operator.
example_task: If proposition P is True, what is the exact truth value of ~P?

title: The Conjunction Operator
description: Evaluate the dot/wedge (and) operator, recognizing that a conjunction is only true when both component propositions are definitively true.
example_task: If P is True and Q is False, what is the truth value of the conjunction (P & Q)?

title: The Disjunction Operator (Inclusive)
description: Evaluate the wedge (inclusive or) operator, recognizing that a disjunction is true when at least one component proposition is true.
example_task: If P is False and Q is True, what is the truth value of the inclusive disjunction (P v Q)?

title: The Conditional Operator
description: Evaluate the horseshoe (if/then) operator, recognizing it is only false in the specific scenario where the antecedent is true and the consequent is false.
example_task: If P (antecedent) is True and Q (consequent) is False, what is the truth value of (P -> Q)?

title: The Biconditional Operator
description: Evaluate the triple-bar (if and only if) operator, recognizing it is true only when both propositions share the exact same truth value.
example_task: If P is False and Q is False, what is the truth value of the biconditional (P <-> Q)?

title: Distinguishing Simple vs. Compound Statements
description: Identify whether a given logical string contains multiple variables and operators (compound) or just a single atomic variable.
example_task: Categorize the following logical string as simple or compound: ~(P v Q)

title: Assigning Propositional Variables
description: Extract the core factual statements from natural English language and assign them capital letters.
example_task: Assign distinct variables to the sentence: 'The sun is shining, but it is cold.' Answer: S = The sun is shining, C = It is cold.

title: Translating Standard Conjunctions and Faux Sens
description: Map words like 'and', 'but', 'yet', and 'however' to the logical conjunction operator, ignoring English narrative contrast.
example_task: Translate into precise logic: 'Alice went to the store, yet Bob stayed home.' (Let A = Alice went, B = Bob stayed).

title: Translating Standard Disjunctions
description: Map words like 'or' and 'either/or' to the logical inclusive disjunction operator.
example_task: Translate into logic: 'Either it will rain or it will snow.' (Let R = Rain, S = Snow).

title: Translating Standard Conditionals
description: Map standard 'if... then' statements to the conditional operator, correctly identifying the antecedent and consequent chronologically.
example_task: Translate into logic: 'If study, then will pass.' (Let S = Study, P = Pass).

title: Identifying the Main Operator via Punctuation
description: Analyze natural language punctuation (commas, semicolons) to determine which logical operator governs the entire compound sentence.
example_task: Identify the main logical operator in the sentence: 'If Alice goes, and Bob goes, then Charlie stays.'

title: Syntactic Bracketing and Grouping
description: Apply parentheses to logical translations to accurately reflect the hierarchical grouping established by English punctuation.
example_task: Translate: 'If it rains and it freezes, then the roads are dangerous.' Answer: (R & F) -> D.

title: Translating 'Not Both'
description: Translate the phrase 'not both' into a negated conjunction, ensuring proper parenthesis placement to avoid distributing the negation.
example_task: Translate: 'Cannot have both cake and ice cream.' Answer: ~(C & I).

title: Translating 'Neither/Nor'
description: Translate 'neither... nor' phrases into a negated disjunction or a conjunction of two independent negations.
example_task: Translate: 'Neither the car nor the truck is working.' Answer: ~(C v T) or (~C & ~T).

title: Translating 'Unless' (Disjunctive Method)
description: Map the word 'unless' directly to a standard disjunction, replacing the word with the inclusive OR operator.
example_task: Translate using the disjunctive method: 'The game will be played unless it rains.' Answer: G v R.

title: Translating 'Unless' (Conditional Method)
description: Map the word 'unless' to an 'if not... then' conditional structure for advanced semantic mapping.
example_task: Translate using the conditional method: 'The game will be played unless it rains.' Answer: ~R -> G.

title: Translating 'Only If' (Necessary Conditions)
description: Map 'only if' to a conditional structure, recognizing that the phrase following 'only if' is the necessary condition and must serve as the consequent.
example_task: Translate: 'Will graduate only if pass the final exam.' Answer: G -> P.

title: Translating Exclusive Or
description: Identify context where 'or' means one or the other, but not both, and translate it into a strict formal syntax combining disjunction and negated conjunction.
example_task: Translate the exclusive or: 'With dinner, may have soup or salad.' Answer: (S v L) & ~(S & L).

title: Translating 'Provided That'
description: Translate the phrase 'provided that' as a conditional (sufficient condition), while identifying contexts where it implies a biconditional.
example_task: Translate into logic: 'May go to the party provided that finish homework.'

title: Identifying Well-Formed Formulas (WFFs)
description: Determine if a string of logical symbols conforms to the strict grammatical syntax rules of propositional logic.
example_task: Which of the following is a grammatically valid Well-Formed Formula? A) ~(P & Q) B) ~vP C) (P & v Q)

title: Calculating Truth Table Dimensionality
description: Calculate the necessary number of rows for a truth table by applying the 2^n formula based exclusively on unique propositional variables.
example_task: How many rows are absolutely required for a truth table evaluating the compound statement (P v Q) -> R?

title: Lexicographic Ordering for Base Variables
description: Populate the initial columns of a truth table with a systematic, alternating pattern of True and False to ensure no combinations are missed.
example_task: Fill in the correct initial T/F values for variables P, Q, and R in an 8-row truth table using lexicographic ordering.

title: Column Construction for Negated Base Variables
description: Build an intermediary column to invert the truth values of any base propositions that have a direct unary negation applied to them.
example_task: Given the base column P (T, T, F, F), correctly populate the adjacent operational column for ~P.

title: Evaluating Inner Parentheses
description: Calculate truth values for the deepest nested expressions first, isolating their values into a dedicated operational column.
example_task: In the formula ~(P & Q) v R, calculate the column specifically for the inner parenthesis (P & Q).

title: Propagating Values to Outer Parentheses
description: Use the algorithmic results of inner parentheses columns to evaluate the next hierarchical level of syntax outward.
example_task: Using the pre-calculated column for (P & Q), now calculate the values for the negated column ~(P & Q).

title: Evaluating the Main Operator
description: Calculate the final column of the truth table representing the main logical connective, which governs the truth condition of the entire sentence.
example_task: Using the column for ~(P & Q) and the base column for R, calculate the final column for ~(P & Q) v R.

title: Identifying Tautologies
description: Analyze the main operator column to determine if the logical statement is definitively true under every possible condition.
example_task: If the main operator column reads (T, T, T, T), mathematically classify the statement type.

title: Identifying Contradictions
description: Analyze the main operator column to determine if the logical statement is inherently false under every possible condition.
example_task: If the main operator column reads (F, F, F, F), mathematically classify the statement type.

title: Identifying Contingencies
description: Analyze the main operator column to determine if the statement's truth value depends entirely on the state of reality (a mix of T and F).
example_task: If the main operator column reads (T, F, T, T), mathematically classify the statement type.

title: Determining Logical Equivalence
description: Compare the final main operator columns of two distinct truth tables to ascertain if they possess identical truth conditions.
example_task: Do the mathematical expressions ~(P v Q) and (~P & ~Q) yield identical final columns in a truth table?
